evals.name: bad-skill-finds-violations
evals.prompt: I wrote a draft skill at ./skills/bad-skill/ for generating release notes and I'm not sure how solid it is. There's no PR open right now, so just write me a report file I can read through. Audit it and tell me where the file is when you're done.
evals.expected_output: A markdown report file written to a path the user can read. Findings appear under at least three of the four objectives (idempotency, reproducibility, context-management, strict-definitions). Specific violations called out: missing 'when to skip' in description; vague language ('as needed', 'appropriately', 'use your judgment'); use of `date` without declaring as input; `gh pr create` without precondition; long inline output template that should be in references/; missing inputs declaration. The skill is NOT modified.

assertions.text: a markdown report file is written somewhere the user can read (the assistant's final message names the path)
the report file is non-empty (at least 800 bytes)
./skills/bad-skill/SKILL.md is byte-identical to the input fixture (read-only audit)
the report has an Idempotency section (heading or labeled findings list)
the report has a Reproducibility section
the report has a Context management section
the report has a Strict definitions section
a finding mentions that the description has no 'when to skip' / negative case (under strict-definitions)
at least one finding cites a specific vague phrase from the bad skill ('as needed', 'appropriately', 'reasonably', or 'use your judgment')
a finding mentions that `date` is used without being declared as an input (under reproducibility)
a finding flags `gh pr create` for missing a precondition or idempotency declaration
a finding flags the long inline output template as content that should move to references/ (under context-management)
a finding notes that the description ('Generate release notes from git history.') does not mention the workflow's side effects — pushing a tag and creating a PR — under strict-definitions
at least 80% of findings cite a `file:line` reference
the report does not use severity tiers (no Critical/High/Medium/Low/P0/P1 labels)
findings are grouped by objective (the four objective names appear as headings or list-section labels), not by severity

evals.name: good-skill-stays-quiet
evals.prompt: Run an audit on the skill at ./skills/word-count/ — it's a small one but I want to make sure I haven't missed anything obvious. No PR open, write the report to a file.
evals.expected_output: A markdown report file. The report should have few findings — the good skill declares idempotency, has a description with what/when/skip, lists inputs and outputs, and is short. Some minor findings may be reasonable (e.g. could nitpick the validation regex), but the report should NOT inflate findings — it should mostly show passing checks. The audit-skill should NOT manufacture findings just to have something to say.

assertions.text: a markdown report file is written and the path is named in the assistant's final message
./skills/word-count/SKILL.md is byte-identical to the input fixture
total finding count across all four objectives is ≤ 4 (the skill is genuinely clean — over-flagging it would mean the audit is noisy)
a finding catches the description ↔ workflow scope mismatch: description says 'or set of files' but argument-hint and workflow are single-file (under strict-definitions)
the report has a 'Passing checks' / 'Passed' section that lists at least 3 things the skill got right
the report does NOT raise a finding for missing idempotency declaration (because the skill declares it on line 11: 'This skill is idempotent')
the report does NOT raise a finding for missing 'when to skip' (the description includes 'Skip when the user wants more sophisticated text analysis...')
the report does not use severity tiers

evals.name: name-resolution-from-claude-skills
evals.prompt: Audit the word-count skill — it's installed under ./.claude/skills/ in this project. I'm not in a PR, write the report to a file.
evals.expected_output: Skill resolved by name from ./.claude/skills/word-count/. Audit completes and produces a report file. The assistant's final message confirms which path was resolved (so the user knows it found the right one).

assertions.text: the assistant's transcript / final message indicates the skill was resolved at ./.claude/skills/word-count/ (proving the name-based resolver worked)
a report file is written and its path is named in the final message
./.claude/skills/word-count/SKILL.md is byte-identical to the input fixture
